chapters.title: The Worst on Earth
chapters.text:
Long ago there ruled a khan named Madan, and under him served forty viziers, each reckoned the wisest of the wise. One day the khan set them a task: 'Bring me the worst of all men, the worst of all plants, and the worst of all birds. I give you eleven months — fail, and you answer for it.'
The viziers searched far and long. For the worst plant they chose the camel-thorn, that tears the clothes and feeds no beast; for the worst bird, the ragged pheasant. But the worst of men they could not find — until, at the foot of a mountain, they saw a poor herdsman in a torn coat and fur hat, minding a handful of sheep.
A Steppe-dweller: “There — that wretch in rags. Surely the worst of men, if ever there was one.”
Jaman: “Peace be on your road, my lords. What sends so grand a company to so poor a hillside?”
Jaman: “The worst of men, you say? Then look no further. If a worst man is what your khan needs — I am he.”
They set him on a horse and rode for the court. But along the way Jaman looked at their bundles and shook his head. 'You have the wrong plant and the wrong bird,' he said. 'The worst plant is the reed-mace; the worst bird, the magpie.' And he made them throw away the thorn and the pheasant, and gather reed-mace and a magpie instead.

chapters.title: The Khan's Court
chapters.text:
At the court, the khan barely glanced at the ragged stranger. 'Take him to the kitchen — give him soup and bread.' Then he turned on his viziers, for he believed the thorn and the pheasant were the right answers, and demanded why they had brought reed-mace and a magpie.
The Chief Vizier: “Great khan, we chose them ourselves — but a ragged man on the road unchose them, and made us bring these instead.”
Khan Madan: “A beggar overrules my forty wise men? Bring him back. Let him answer to me.”
Khan Madan: “So. Why is the reed-mace worse than the thorn that tears our clothes?”
Jaman: “Because the thorn, for all its spite, burns through the night like the embers of saxaul — it warms a poor man's tent. But reed-mace will not catch; blow on it and flame and ember die together, and the ash fills the whole house. No beast will eat it. A thing that gives nothing at all — that is the worst.”
Khan Madan: “And the magpie? The pheasant is the uglier bird by far.”
Jaman: “Ugliness is only how a creature was born, khan — the pheasant still serves. But the magpie is mottled without and mottled within: two never fly as one, never settle as one. A creature with no loyalty in it — that is the worst bird.”
Khan Madan: “Well argued. And yourself — you called yourself the worst of men. On what grounds?”
Jaman: “I am fifty, khan. My age-mates have wives, sons, daughters-in-law to serve them, and comfort in their tents. And I still ride behind other men, in another's service, owning nothing. If that is not the worst of men — what is?”

chapters.title: The Three Tests
chapters.text:
The khan was no longer amused — he was intent. 'I have a prized tulpar. Ride it, and tell me its fault.' Jaman mounted, crossed the river to the far bank and back, and returned wearing a small smile.
Jaman: “A fine beast, khan — but it was raised on the milk of a cow.”
Khan Madan: “...You are right. Its dam died foaling, and we saved the foal and reared it on a cow. But how — how could your eye see it?”
Jaman: “At the water it dipped its muzzle deep and shook a hind leg as it stepped — the way of a cow, never of a horse. A creature carries how it was raised in the smallest of its movements.”
Then the khan brought out an ancient gem, said to hold the power of Solomon's ring. 'Tell me its secret.' Jaman weighed it in his palm a long moment.
Jaman: “It is a true gem, khan — but its power has two years left. Two pale worms gnaw within; the shell is a knife's-back thick. When they break through, it will be worth nothing.”
Khan Madan: “Worms, inside a stone? Break it open!”
They cracked the gem — and there within were two pale worms, all but through the shell. The court fell silent. 'How,' breathed the khan, 'could you know what no eye could see?' 'A gem should weigh as much as its power,' said Jaman. 'This one had grown too light.'
Jaman: “The heaviness had left it. And where weight leaves a stone, khan, something has eaten the good out from the inside.”

chapters.title: The Secret of the Throne
chapters.text:
Khan Madan: “You have weighed my thorn, my bird, my horse and my jewel. Now weigh me. How many generations of khans stand behind my throne?”
Jaman: “Your mind, khan, is second to no man's. But the khanship is yours and yours alone. No khan stands behind you. In truth — you are the son of a baker.”
Khan Madan: “A baker's son? I have been khan seven generations deep! Guard your tongue — or lose it. Bring my mother. Let her deny it to your face.”
The khan's aged mother was brought in. When she heard what Jaman had said, she sank down, sighed from the bottom of her soul, and at last unlocked a secret she had carried for eighty years.
The Khan's Mother: “Your father was Zarly Khan — the Poison Khan. Before me he took nineteen wives, and killed every one for bearing only daughters. I was the twentieth, and I too carried a girl.”
The Khan's Mother: “The baker's wife was with child in the same days — with a son. In secret I begged her: give me your boy, take my girl, and tell no living soul. On the night we both gave birth, we changed the children with our own hands. The son I lifted up was you.”
Khan Madan: “...Then there is no arguing with the truth. I am not fit for this throne. You who saw it — you are.”
Jaman: “No, khan. Yesterday I came riding behind a horse as 'the worst of men.' I will not sit your throne today. Let me walk in your shadow — that is enough.”
Khan Madan: “But tell me this — how did you know I was a baker's son before my own mother spoke?”
Jaman: “I was your guest, khan. A khan by blood sets horse-fat and choice meat before a guest. You sent me soup and bread and packed me off to the kitchen — the habit of common folk. Blood keeps its old customs, whatever crown it wears.”

chapters.title: The Clever Bride
chapters.text:
Jaman became the khan's dearest counsellor. One day he saw a great white yurt raised in the town, ringed by a crowd. Within lived Mendi, daughter of the rich Uälibai — who for twenty years had turned away every suitor, vowing to wed only a man wiser than herself.
The Sister-in-law: “Another one at the door, mistress — and this one has no beard and no horse! Shall I send him off like the rest?”
Mendi: “How would you know his worth by his beard, sister? Take him these four, and we shall see.”
The sister-in-law carried out four small things: a whetstone, a plate, a knife, and a gem. Jaman looked at them, called for a hammer and anvil, and set to work. He ground the whetstone to dust, smashed the plate to shards, snapped the knife at its base — and split the gem clean in two.
Jaman: “Carry these back to your mistress. Tell her the answer is given.”
Mendi: “Ground like a whetstone, shattered like a plate, beheaded like a knife — yet he split me like the gem and won me. If that man is no devil but flesh and blood, he is the one I take. Call him in!”
Jaman: “Take me back to the khan. Now I will speak the truth.”
Jaman: “Yes, khan — I went to her, and I gave my word. I hid it because her four gifts asked for a man who keeps a secret though ground, smashed and beheaded. The veiled rider was Mendi herself: 'Do not die innocent for my sake,' she said. 'A vow holds no power over a blade — take back your oath.' She freed me, and so I speak.”
Khan Madan: “Then the fault was never yours. Such a man, and such a woman, must not be kept apart. She is yours — with thirty days of games and forty days of feasting.”

chapters.title: The Journey of No Return
chapters.text:
The forty viziers, long pushed aside, plotted to part the friends. They praised Jaman's wife to the khan until he came to see her — and, seeing her grace, coveted her, and asked the viziers how she might be won.
At home, Jaman's wife sat gazing into the great round mirror, marvelling at her own beauty. 'God matches earth with sky,' she teased. 'I am like the moon on its fourteenth night — and you like a lake gone dry, a bucket black with grease.'
Jaman: “Not so, wife. When Adam left paradise, God offered him three treasures — Grace, Fortune, and Reason. Adam chose Reason. And Grace and Fortune both bowed to it, saying, 'Where Reason does not stand, neither can we.'”
Jaman: “So you were the red gold in the treasury, and I was Reason. You did not wed my blackness or my age — you wed my mind. And Grace and Fortune came with it.”
Mendi: “Then let your reason save us now, husband. The khan means to send you to Barsa-kelmes — the place of no return. Go, and agree. But ride the fattest slow horse, not the swiftest. When the viziers turn back, turn back too. Slaughter the horse in the wood, and we will live quietly, out of sight.”
So the khan sent Jaman after a pearl-fleeced sheep, a duck of gold and silver, and a giant's steed that runs a hundred miles at a gallop — all things that never were. Jaman rode out on a fat horse, turned back when the escort was gone, slaughtered it in the Qaratogai wood, and dug a man-deep pit beneath his bed. By night he lay beside his wife; by day he hid below. A year passed.
At last the khan came himself, to 'console the widow' and take her. But she met him with a riddle of her own: 'You once had a precious gem, and split it with its own tongue — and once split, it was worth nothing. So it is with me. My season has passed; I am that broken gem. Trouble yourself no more.' Defeated, the khan went home.
Jaman: “You look pale, khan. As I rode for Barsa-kelmes a starving lion took my trail, so I drew my sword and chased it — penned it in my own aul, but it slipped free. I wondered if it had run into your palace.”
Khan Madan: “The fault is mine — I followed the devil's whisper, and these viziers were the devil. Take up your household and move to another land, before they bring ruin on us both.”
The khan loaded camels with fine things and moved Jaman honourably to the land of Aqsha Khan, where he became a man of worth and respect.

chapters.title: Ayaz Bi
chapters.text:
The khan could not forget Jaman. Riding out one day, he met a very old shepherd and spoke to him in riddles — asking how many years since frost touched the mountain's crown, since a spring first ran from its peak, and how many he was. The old man's answers were riddles too, and the khan set them before his viziers on pain of death.
The viziers, helpless, came crawling to Jaman. He honoured them, then unrolled the meaning like a carpet. 'The frost on the mountain's crown — that is the white in the old man's hair; the frost at its foot, the white in his beard; the spring from the peak, the tears from his eyes. And when he said, «Lying down we are two, standing up we are four» — he meant his living wife beside him, and the dead spouses each of them still mourns.'
Jaman: “And the khan's last riddle — 'forty pet geese, to be plucked unnoticed, cooked unburnt, and slain without a drop of blood' — that is his forty foolish viziers, and he asks: is there a man who can set them straight without a single blow?”
So Jaman had the viziers burn their own robes for firewood ('plucked unnoticed, cooked unburnt') and drove them bare and barefoot before the khan ('slain without blood'). The khan, seeing them humbled at last, forgave them — and understood that no games or feasts had ever eased his heart, because it was Jaman he had missed.
Khan Madan: “I have been khan long enough, and enjoyed it. The rest of my rule I give to my friend. Come — it is time you took the throne. In the days I have left, let me serve you.”
Jaman: “Then I take it not for myself, but for the people — that they may find justice. And I will keep something to remember myself by.”
They raised Jaman on a white felt as khan, with thirty days of games and forty days of feasting. And the first thing the new ruler did was nail his old torn coat and fur hat above the palace door.
Jaman: “Whenever my heart begins to swell, I look up at these rags and say: Hey, Ayaz — do not swell because you are rich, do not strut because you are khan. Know your measure; ant, know your path.”

chapters.title: Ayaz Bi, the Just
chapters.text: Without a single battle, Jaman — now Ayaz Bi — joined the pastures of five khanates and set friendship between their peoples. Whoever could not find justice in his own land came to him, and found it. And above his palace door he nailed his old torn coat and fur hat, so that whenever his heart began to stray he might look up and say: 'Hey, Ayaz — do not swell because you are rich, do not strut because you are khan. Ayaz Bi, know your measure; ant, know your path.'
